query: how do I rotate an expired API key without downtime?

chunks.text: Welcome to the platform. This guide covers account creation, billing tiers, and how to invite teammates to your workspace from the settings panel.
Our dashboard provides usage charts, monthly invoices, and a notifications center. You can export charts as PNG or CSV for reporting.
Support is available 24/7 via chat. Enterprise customers also get a dedicated success manager and a private Slack channel.
The mobile app mirrors the web dashboard and supports push notifications for budget alerts and weekly summaries.
API keys are created in Settings > Developers. Each key is scoped to a single project and can be named for easy identification.

To rotate an expired API key without downtime, first create a new key, deploy it to all services, confirm traffic is flowing on the new key, then revoke the old key. Overlapping keys avoid any gap in authentication.
Rate limits are enforced per key. The default is 600 requests per minute; contact sales to raise the ceiling for production workloads.
Key rotation can be automated with the CLI: `platform keys rotate --grace 24h` issues a new key and keeps the previous one valid for a 24 hour overlap window before automatic revocation.
Webhooks deliver events to your endpoint with an HMAC signature. Verify the signature header before trusting any payload.

query: why did checkout latency spike on Tuesday?

chunks.text: The frontend team shipped a new product page with lazy-loaded images and a redesigned navigation bar this sprint.
Marketing launched a spring campaign that drove a 30% increase in homepage visits, mostly from social referrals.
Checkout latency spiked on Tuesday because a database connection pool was exhausted after a deploy lowered the pool size from 50 to 10 connections.
Our analytics pipeline batches events every five minutes and writes them to the warehouse for the BI dashboards.
The design system was updated with new color tokens and accessible focus states across all buttons and form fields.
We use feature flags to gradually roll out risky changes and can disable a flag instantly if metrics regress.

Root cause of the Tuesday checkout latency spike: the connection pool change combined with the campaign traffic caused requests to queue for database connections, adding 800ms p95 latency until the pool was raised back to 50.
The incident was mitigated by reverting the pool size and adding an alert on connection saturation above 80%.

query: what is the refund window for annual plans?

chunks.text: Plans are billed monthly or annually. Annual plans receive a 20% discount compared to paying month to month.
Refund window: annual plans are eligible for a full refund within 30 days of purchase. After 30 days, annual plans are non-refundable but remain active until the end of the term.
You can upgrade or downgrade at any time; changes are prorated against your current billing period automatically.
Payment methods include major credit cards and, for enterprise, invoicing with net-30 terms.
Taxes are calculated at checkout based on your billing address and applicable local regulations.
Monthly plans can be cancelled anytime and simply stop renewing at the next cycle; no partial refunds are issued for monthly plans.
Failed payments trigger three retry attempts over a week before the subscription is paused and access is restricted.

query: how does the retriever handle duplicate documents?

chunks.text: The ingestion service accepts PDFs, HTML, and Markdown, then splits them into overlapping passages for indexing.
Embeddings are computed with a sentence-transformer model and stored in a vector database with cosine similarity search.
The retriever returns the top-k passages by similarity, which are then re-ranked by a cross-encoder before being sent to the LLM.
Duplicate documents are handled by content hashing at ingestion: identical passages share a single vector, and near-duplicates are collapsed using a similarity threshold of 0.95.
The cross-encoder re-ranker improves precision by scoring each query-passage pair jointly rather than independently.
Passages carry metadata such as source URL, section heading, and ingestion timestamp for filtering and citation.
At query time, a deduplication pass removes near-identical retrieved passages so the context window is not wasted on redundant copies of the same content.
Caching of query embeddings reduces latency for repeated questions and lowers embedding API costs.

query: what permissions are required to delete a production cluster?

chunks.text: Clusters can be created from the console, the CLI, or via the Terraform provider using a service account token.
Each cluster belongs to a project, and projects are grouped into organizations for billing and access control.
Deleting a production cluster requires the Cluster Admin role plus a second approval from an organization Owner; the action is also gated behind a typed confirmation of the cluster name.
Node pools can autoscale between a minimum and maximum count based on pending workloads and resource pressure.
Read-only viewers can see cluster status and logs but cannot modify any cluster configuration or workloads.
Editors can deploy workloads, change autoscaling settings, and manage secrets within a project's clusters.
Audit logs capture every cluster create, update, and delete event with the actor, timestamp, and source IP.

query: how do I configure exponential backoff for retries?

chunks.text: The SDK is available for Python, TypeScript, and Go, with consistent method names across all three languages.
Authentication uses a bearer token passed in the Authorization header on every request to the API.
Responses are paginated with a cursor; pass the returned cursor to fetch the next page of results.
Errors return a structured JSON body with a machine-readable code and a human-readable message field.

Configure exponential backoff by setting the retry policy: an initial delay, a multiplier, and a maximum delay. For example, base 200ms, multiplier 2, max 10s yields delays of 200ms, 400ms, 800ms, and so on, capped at 10 seconds.
Idempotency keys let you safely retry write requests without creating duplicate resources on the server.
Timeouts default to 30 seconds per request and can be overridden per call through the client options object.
Add jitter to exponential backoff to avoid thundering-herd retries: randomize each delay within its computed window so many clients do not retry in lockstep after an outage.

query: what data is stored when a user signs up?

chunks.text: The signup flow asks for an email address and password, with optional single sign-on through Google or GitHub.
After signup, users are guided through a short onboarding checklist to connect their first data source.

On signup we store the user's email, a salted password hash, account creation timestamp, and the signup source. We do not store the raw password, and payment details are tokenized by our payment processor rather than stored directly.
Profile pictures are optional and served from a CDN with a content hash in the URL for cache-busting.
Email verification is required before a user can invite teammates or create API keys.
Users can delete their account at any time, which triggers a 30-day soft-delete window before permanent erasure.

query: how are evaluation scores aggregated across test cases?

chunks.text: The evaluation harness loads a suite of test cases, each with an input prompt and an expected behavior specification.
Each test case is run against the model under test, and the raw output is captured along with latency and token usage.
Graders can be exact-match, regex, or an LLM-as-judge that returns a pass/fail with a short rationale.
Per-case scores are aggregated into a suite score by taking the mean pass rate, with optional weighting so that critical safety cases count more than cosmetic ones.
Results are written to a JSON report and rendered as an HTML dashboard with sortable columns and trend charts.

Aggregation also computes a weighted mean across categories, then reports a confidence interval using bootstrap resampling so small suites do not over-state a score difference between two model versions.
Regression detection flags any category whose score dropped beyond a threshold compared to the previous run.
